items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: When you flex your elbow, which muscle is the agonist?
items.why: Students confuse agonist (the prime mover doing the action) with antagonist (the opposing muscle that relaxes). In elbow flexion, biceps is the agonist; triceps is the antagonist (and vice versa for extension).

items.q: What causes muscle soreness 1-2 days after intense exercise?
items.correct: Microscopic muscle damage (DOMS), not lactic acid.
items.misconception: Lactic acid still in the muscles.
items.why: Students believe the myth that lactic acid causes soreness. Lactate clears within an hour. Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is caused by microtrauma from eccentric contractions, peaking 24-72 hours later.
It's Performance peaks at moderate arousal. “Performance rises linearly with arousal” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Dominant response is correct (experts). “Skill is novice” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If optimal arousal for archery is 4/10 and for boxing 7/10, mean optimal arousal across the two (out of 10) is?
items.why: The real figure is 5.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
